title: Sectional conflict: regional differences
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on sectional conflict.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below.

![Political cartoon showing Irish immigrant coming to America in ragged clothing and later returning to Ireland for a visit as a well-to-do man.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5893292459b4fbcfac8268f6a6e2aba6.jpg)
“Coming to America; Returning for a Visit.” 1854. Source: [New York Public Library](https://digitalcollections.nypl.org/items/510d47e1-37c1-a3d9-e040-e00a18064a99)

**Which of the following statements best describes the perspective expressed in the image about immigration?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Irish immigrants could improve their financial situation by coming to America
Irish immigrants were more loyal to the Catholic church than to the United States
Irish immigrants were incapable of assimilating into American culture
Irish immigrants were ideal recruits for the US Army during the Civil War

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6f2155dcfd69b3b70c38b4ebd5c2e607.jpg)
Thomas Nast, “The Usual Irish Way of Doing Things,” 1871. Source: [American Social History Project](https://herb.ashp.cuny.edu/items/show/640)

**The image most closely reflects which of the following developments in the political climate in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the emergence of a strong anti-Catholic nativist movement
the embrace of temperance by the Democratic Party
the decision to end military Reconstruction in the South
the growing influence of anti-imperialist newspapers

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6f2155dcfd69b3b70c38b4ebd5c2e607.jpg)
Thomas Nast, “The Usual Irish Way of Doing Things,” 1871. Source: [American Social History Project](https://herb.ashp.cuny.edu/items/show/640)

**The political cartoon was intended to:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: warn Americans about the threat of Irish immigrants.
encourage Irish assimilation into the United States.
argue for the ratification of a temperance amendment.
defend the institution of slavery in the South.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“If we do not exclude slavery from the territories, it will exclude us . . . Where slave labor strikes its roots deep into the soil of a territory, free labor will not grow, but perish. We are all personally interested in this question, not indirectly and remotely, as in a mere political abstraction, but directly, pecuniarily and selfishly.” 

>-Speech given by Oliver P. Morton, 1860

**Which of the following groups would have been most likely to support the views expressed in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Free Soil Party
Abolitionists
The Know-Nothing Party
Transcendentalists

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“If we do not exclude slavery from the territories, it will exclude us . . . Where slave labor strikes its roots deep into the soil of a territory, free labor will not grow, but perish. We are all personally interested in this question, not indirectly and remotely, as in a mere political abstraction, but directly, pecuniarily and selfishly.” 

>-Speech given by Oliver P. Morton, 1860

**The speech excerpted most directly reflected a growing belief after 1850 that:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: slavery was incompatible with free labor.
territorial expansion would ruin the United States.
slave labor was detrimental to agriculture.
northern workers were “wage slaves.”

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“If we do not exclude slavery from the territories, it will exclude us . . . Where slave labor strikes its roots deep into the soil of a territory, free labor will not grow, but perish. We are all personally interested in this question, not indirectly and remotely, as in a mere political abstraction, but directly, pecuniarily and selfishly.” 

>-Source: Speech given by Oliver P. Morton, 1860

**Which of the following was a major difference in economic development between the North and the South in the 1850s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The North’s economy relied on free labor, while the South’s economy relied on enslaved labor.
The North had a manufacturing economy, while the South had a mixed industrial and agricultural economy.
The North’s economy struggled under tariffs, while the South’s economy flourished with the enactment of tariffs.
The North’s economy relied heavily on foreign trade, while the South’s economy relied only on domestic trade.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“I, John Brown, am now quite certain that the crimes of this guilty land can never be purged away but with blood. I had, as I now think, vainly flattered myself that without very much bloodshed, it might be done.” 

>-Source: Last written words before Brown’s execution, 1859

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the growing willingness of abolitionists to resort to violence
the influence of the Second Great Awakening on the abolitionist movement
the massive carnage of the Battle of Antietam
the response of pro-slavery politicians to the Dred Scott decision

assessment_items.question_content:
>“What, to the American slave, is your 4th of July? I answer: a day that reveals to him, more than all other days in the year, the gross injustice and cruelty to which he is the constant victim. To him, your celebration is a sham . . . a thin veil to cover up crimes which would disgrace a nation of savages. There is not a nation on the earth guilty of practices, more shocking and bloody, than are the people of these United States, at this very hour.”

>-Source: Frederick Douglass, “What to the Slave is the Fourth of July?” 1852.

**Which of the following movements expressed ideas most similar to the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the abolitionist movement
the free-soil movement
the temperance movement
the women’s rights movement

assessment_items.question_content:
>“What, to the American slave, is your 4th of July? I answer: a day that reveals to him, more than all other days in the year, the gross injustice and cruelty to which he is the constant victim. To him, your celebration is a sham . . . a thin veil to cover up crimes which would disgrace a nation of savages. There is not a nation on the earth guilty of practices, more shocking and bloody, than are the people of these United States, at this very hour.”

>-Frederick Douglass, “What to the Slave is the Fourth of July?” 1852.

**In the decades leading up to Douglass’s speech, the abolitionist movement focused its energies most strongly on which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: moral arguments against the institution of slavery
violent reprisals against slaveowners
legislative change through the Republican Party
constitutional amendments ensuring black citizenship

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The principal reason why we abhor so much the term slavery is, the base cruelty with which some tyrant slaveholders . . . have treated their slaves. Hence we are very apt to use as synonymous terms, slavery, cruelty, tyranny, and oppression. . . . Does any one really believe that a man cannot treat his slaves kindly, tenderly, and affectionately? If any one thinks it possible, then let not, for the future, the terms slavery  and cruelty be inseparably united.” 

>-William Willcocks Sleigh, *Abolitionism Exposed!*, 1832

**Which of the following developments from the mid-nineteenth century emerged from ideas most similar to those expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the defense of slavery as a positive good by white southerners
the manumission of enslaved African Americans by slaveholders
the creation of a colony in Liberia by emancipated African Americans
the critique of industrial labor as wage slavery by northern factory workers

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The principal reason why we abhor so much the term slavery is, the base cruelty with which some tyrant slaveholders . . . have treated their slaves. Hence we are very apt to use as synonymous terms, slavery, cruelty, tyranny, and oppression. . . . Does any one really believe that a man cannot treat his slaves kindly, tenderly, and affectionately? If any one thinks it possible, then let not, for the future, the terms slavery  and cruelty be inseparably united.” 

>-William Willcocks Sleigh, *Abolitionism Exposed!*, 1832

**Which of the following groups would have been likely to support the author’s views expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: southern Democrats
northern Whigs
the Free Soil Party
the Know-Nothing Party

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The Negro slaves of the South are the happiest, and, in some sense, the freest people in the world. The children and the aged and infirm work not at all, and yet have all the comforts and necessaries of life provided for them. They enjoy liberty, because they are oppressed neither by care nor labor. . . . The free laborer must work or starve. He is more of a slave than the Negro because he works longer and harder for less allowance than the slave and has no holiday, because the cares of life with him begin when its labors end. He has no liberty, and not a single right.”

>-Source: Frederick Law Olmsted, *The Cotton Kingdom: A Traveller’s Observations on Cotton and Slavery in the American Slave States,* 1853–1861

**The ideas about slavery expressed in the excerpt are most consistent with which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the positive good theory
the free soil movement
the ideas of abolitionism
the principle of popular sovereignty
